title: A heart-to-heart
desc: Open up about a hard day
setting: You've had a rough day. Roberto can tell something's off and asks about it.

steps.speaker: Roberto
steps.es: Te veo medio raro. ¿Qué te pasa?
steps.en: You seem a bit off. What's wrong?

options.es: Tuve un mal día, la verdad.
options.en: I had a bad day, honestly.
options.feedback: Open and natural — 'la verdad' (honestly) makes it feel real and trusting.
We all say it, but he can tell. It's okay to let a friend in.

options.es: Estoy cansado y un poco triste.
options.en: I'm tired and a little sad.
options.feedback: Naming feelings directly — exactly the Phase 2 leap. Honest and clear.

options.es: Perdí mi teléfono y llegué tarde al trabajo.
options.en: I lost my phone and got to work late.
options.feedback: Two past-tense verbs telling a real story — 'perdí' and 'llegué.' Strong.

options.en: A lot of things went wrong today.
options.feedback: Nice — 'salieron mal' (went wrong) is a useful, natural phrase.
Valid to feel that way — though he's offering an ear, and that's worth something.

steps.speaker: Roberto
steps.es: Uy, qué mala onda. ¿Y cómo te sentís ahora?
steps.en: Oof, that's rough. And how do you feel now?

options.feedback: Honest about the feeling AND grateful for the talk. Beautifully balanced.

options.es: Un poco mejor, gracias por escuchar.
options.en: A little better, thanks for listening.
options.feedback: Warm and real — acknowledging his support is exactly right.
Honest — and that's okay. A good friend won't rush you to feel better.

steps.speaker: Roberto
steps.es: Tranquilo, hermano. Mañana es otro día.

options.feedback: Returning 'hermano' seals the bond — that's deep friendship in Nica terms.
Touching and sincere. This is what Phase 2 is all about — real connection.
He's being a true friend — a warmer reply honors that better.
